=== Employee Spotlight: Nic van Vliet ===

Meet Nic van Vliet, AI Project Lead at KickstartAI. With a background in startups, product operations at Google, and his own AI agency, he now focuses on moving AI projects move from idea to adoption. We spoke with Nic about his path into AI, what motivates him in his work, and where he sees the technology making the biggest difference.

>>> What’s your role at KickstartAI? <<<

I’m an AI Project Lead. My role is about helping move projects forward together with our tech team, our partners, and everyone else involved. So it’s a mix of making sure things keep progressing, aligning the right people, and turning ideas into working products.

Right now, I’m leading three projects: one with ING focused on using GenAI to support financial health, and with NS I’m working on detecting unattended objects at train stations, as well as exploring how AI can help improve train energy consumption.

>>> What’s your background? <<<

My background is quite diverse. I studied organization and venture management in the US, and began my career working with early-stage startups. I returned to grad school to study international development and economics, before eventually joining Google, where I spent several years in product operations. In 2024, I moved back into the startup world and, more recently, ventured into AI and built my own AI agency, before joining KickstartAI.

>>> What drives you in your work? <<<

I think we’re at the beginning of a major technological transition that will impact everyone. What drives me is making sure people are as informed as possible about it, understanding both the strengths and the limitations of AI. Not just to use it in their own work, but also to be able to ask the right questions, whether that’s within their organization, or more broadly in society.

For me, a big part of the work is helping bring that understanding into projects and conversations with partners, so that we adopt the technology in a thoughtful and responsible way, rather than just letting it happen to us.

>>> What has stood out to you most about working at KickstartAI so far? <<<

Definitely the people and the shared sense of purpose! And another thing I really enjoy is our way of working. A good example of that for me is the ING Challenge, which I’m really proud of. We brought together 15 participants from the partner companies and worked together to build a generative AI chatbot prototype for ING focused on financial health –  all in just two weeks!

>>> Where do you see AI making the biggest difference in the future? <<<

Climate change is definitely one area where AI can have a real impact. It’s fascinating how much it can speed up material discovery, for example.  And it’s not hard to imagine how new materials could prove useful for fields like energy, conductivity, or carbon capture. Of course, humans still need to test and apply those in the real world, but the fact that AI can uncover those kinds of possibilities at an unprecedented scale makes a big difference.

>>> What’s your advice to people considering a career in AI? <<<

belief: People should be as informed as possible about AI, including both its strengths and limitations.
source_quote: making sure people are as informed as possible about it, understanding both the strengths and the limitations of AI
source_text:
I’m always optimizing my route from A to B. For example, I figured out the fastest route from our office to the lunch canteen, and I was surprised that people who’d been there much longer than me didn’t know about it.

belief: Hands-on experimentation and creating with AI tools are essential for truly understanding AI and preparing for a career in the field.
source_quote: the best thing you can do is not just consume it, but actually create with it

My biggest advice would be to just jump in and start using it. And by that, I don’t just mean ChatGPT – I mean trying different tools, experimenting with different LLMs, building small things yourself, and seeing how they actually work in practice. For me, that hands-on experimentation made AI feel much less abstract and helped me understand it a lot better. If you’re considering a career in AI, I think the best thing you can do is not just consume it, but actually create with it.

And as for project managers in the field, I think an important quality is being able to see a few steps ahead. The more you collaborate, the more you talk to different people, and the more perspectives you bring together, the better you get at anticipating what’s coming next – and in AI especially, that’s really important.

>>> Fun fact about you? <<<

I used to perform in musicals in high school – I even played Tony in West Side Story !

=== Why focus and technology go hand in hand ===

>>> ING's COO Risk Leon Dusée on AI in risk management, why pilots often stall, and how a promising AI solution such as a financial health assistant could help people sooner <<<

By Rutger Huizenga

AI is now on almost every agenda. Not as a gadget, but as a strategic question: where do you apply it, how do you scale it, and how do you prevent experiments from getting stuck in pilot phase? In the interview series "AI Lessons from the Field," we speak with leaders who have to make those choices in practice. Today: Leon Dusée, Global Head Chief Operating Officer Risk at ING. “AI can add value in thousands of places, but if you start everywhere, you often end up nowhere.”

When speaking with Leon Dusée about the future of banking, it quickly becomes clear that he is not only focused on the latest technological gadgets. Although, as COO Risk at ING, he is responsible for the security and stability of a global digital infrastructure, his focus lies on a surprisingly human aspect: making deliberate choices.

“AI can add value in thousands of places,” he says matter-of-factly. “But that is exactly the danger. If you start everywhere, you often end up nowhere.”

It is a lesson he learned in practice at the bank, where he started eight years ago as a management trainee and rose to global leadership of the operations connected to risk management. Technological choices become reality faster when the entire organization has the right focus.

“Everyone sees opportunities in AI, and those opportunities are real. But in a large organization, the temptation is strong to experiment in many places at once. Before long, you get a proliferation of pilots, proofs of concept, and isolated ideas that never truly land in the core process. The art is to say: this is where we will invest, and this is where we will not. That sounds simple, but it is not. Because it also means saying no to good ideas, to initiatives that may seem smarter or more efficient in themselves.”

--- Critical thinking ---

belief: Project managers in AI should be able to look a few steps ahead and integrate diverse perspectives to anticipate what is coming next.
source_quote: an important quality is being able to see a few steps ahead
source_text:
My biggest advice would be to just jump in and start using it. And by that, I don’t just mean ChatGPT – I mean trying different tools, experimenting with different LLMs, building small things yourself, and seeing how they actually work in practice. For me, that hands-on experimentation made AI feel much less abstract and helped me understand it a lot better. If you’re considering a career in AI, I think the best thing you can do is not just consume it, but actually create with it.

And as for project managers in the field, I think an important quality is being able to see a few steps ahead. The more you collaborate, the more you talk to different people, and the more perspectives you bring together, the better you get at anticipating what’s coming next – and in AI especially, that’s really important.

>>> Fun fact about you? <<<

I used to perform in musicals in high school – I even played Tony in West Side Story !

>>> ING's COO Risk Leon Dusée on AI in risk management, why pilots often stall, and how a promising AI solution such as a financial health assistant could help people sooner <<<

By Rutger Huizenga

AI is now on almost every agenda. Not as a gadget, but as a strategic question: where do you apply it, how do you scale it, and how do you prevent experiments from getting stuck in pilot phase? In the interview series "AI Lessons from the Field," we speak with leaders who have to make those choices in practice. Today: Leon Dusée, Global Head Chief Operating Officer Risk at ING. “AI can add value in thousands of places, but if you start everywhere, you often end up nowhere.”

When speaking with Leon Dusée about the future of banking, it quickly becomes clear that he is not only focused on the latest technological gadgets. Although, as COO Risk at ING, he is responsible for the security and stability of a global digital infrastructure, his focus lies on a surprisingly human aspect: making deliberate choices.

“AI can add value in thousands of places,” he says matter-of-factly. “But that is exactly the danger. If you start everywhere, you often end up nowhere.”

It is a lesson he learned in practice at the bank, where he started eight years ago as a management trainee and rose to global leadership of the operations connected to risk management. Technological choices become reality faster when the entire organization has the right focus.

“Everyone sees opportunities in AI, and those opportunities are real. But in a large organization, the temptation is strong to experiment in many places at once. Before long, you get a proliferation of pilots, proofs of concept, and isolated ideas that never truly land in the core process. The art is to say: this is where we will invest, and this is where we will not. That sounds simple, but it is not. Because it also means saying no to good ideas, to initiatives that may seem smarter or more efficient in themselves.”

--- Critical thinking ---

belief: Employees must learn not only how to use AI but also how to question it critically, applying expertise rather than blind trust.
source_quote: “You have to learn not only how to use it, but also how to question it... That requires expertise, not blind trust.”
source_text:
In the operations domain of Risk, for which he is responsible, Dusée clearly sees the difference AI can make. Much of the work involves assessing huge volumes of information: plans, validations, controls, and documentation. A large part of that is still manual and time-consuming. According to Dusée, AI can add a great deal of value here, for example by extracting, organizing, and analyzing information more quickly. But for him, the biggest change is not about speed. It is about what AI asks of people.

“AI forces employees to work more critically,” says Dusée. “You have to learn not only how to use it, but also how to question it. Where are the limits? When do you trust the output, and when do you not? What thresholds do you apply? That requires expertise, not blind trust.”

He adds: “The pace of change is high. Two years ago, we were talking about chatbots; now we are already talking about agentic AI and conversational AI. That is why you should not only train employees on today’s tools, but above all teach them how to keep adapting, spot opportunities, and help steer AI adoption.”

That is why ING, he says, is deliberately investing in training, with programs for both employees and leaders. Not because everyone needs to become a technical specialist, but because AI does not work if it remains a toy for a small group of experts.

“Often, someone spots an opportunity within their own scope, for example when creating a report. That is a good start, but for leaders the real work only begins after that: can you apply such a solution more broadly? Not for one report, but for all reports, or perhaps even for the entire process? That requires choices. You cannot do everything at once. Sometimes you have to say very explicitly: these are the six use cases we will focus on this year, and we will leave those other twenty aside for now.”

Continuing to challenge and question yourself is also important when it comes to changing processes through AI, he emphasizes.

“The first reflex is often: how can I add AI while keeping everything else the same? But the more interesting question is: should this process actually be fundamentally redesigned?”

At ING, he sees this, for example, in the manual processes used to collect data and make it available for models. The question is not only which step can be made faster, but whether the entire chain should be redesigned.

“Only then does AI become a strategic question,” he says. “Not because executives are operating the technology themselves, but because they determine what gets priority, how it is embedded and who is responsible for it.”

That, according to Dusée, is where things often go wrong in practice. Not with the technology, but with the organization.

“Many pilots fail because organizations think about day two too late. Teams build something in a safe environment and it works. But only then does the real question arise: where does this land in the organization? In which process? Which team becomes the owner? How do you maintain it? How does it connect to existing systems? If you only start asking those questions after the pilot, you get friction. Other teams have not been involved, the processes do not align, or there is simply no capacity to roll it out. Then something remains stuck in a kind of innovation limbo.”

To prevent this, ING has set up a clear structure for AI: a central analytics team that oversees the portfolio, while concrete ideas can still come from the shop floor.

“That central direction is essential to prevent teams from reinventing the wheel in their own small corner. The aim is to work in an ‘agnostic’ way: a solution that works for one type of reporting should be immediately scalable to all reporting within the bank.”

--- KickstartAI ---

belief: AI should not only be approached as a machine for working more efficiently but also as a means to create societal impact and support people’s needs.
source_quote: If we approach AI only as a machine for working more efficiently, we are selling ourselves short.
source_text:
The questions Dusée encounters within ING are by no means unique to banks, he says. That is precisely why he is active within KickstartAI, the partnership involving organizations including KLM, NS, ING and Ahold Delhaize, which aims to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands. The sectors may differ, but the underlying AI challenges are often much more similar.

“KLM’s market is completely different from ING’s,” he says. “But you encounter the same challenges. From governance and data to the question: how do you move from a proof of concept to a real product?” For organizations, he believes, the value is immediate: learning faster what works and what does not.

For Dusée, KickstartAI also has a broader meaning than knowledge exchange between large companies. He explicitly sees it as a way to create more societal impact. As an example, he mentions an AI tool around financial health that ING is currently exploring  together with KickstartAI, with the aim of eventually investigating how customers can be helped earlier and more accessibly when financial problems are at risk of emerging.

Dusée: “How do you reach people earlier, lower barriers and offer help in a way that matches what people need? I find that at least as relevant. If we approach AI only as a machine for working more efficiently, we are selling ourselves short.”

But for Dusée, the value of KickstartAI extends beyond the partners alone. He sees the network not only as a place where large organizations exchange knowledge, but also as a way to make expertise more broadly available.

“There are so many organizations in the Netherlands, both profit and non-profit, where AI can be incredibly valuable,” he says. “In education, research, and healthcare, for example. But not everyone has the financial resources or capacity to apply AI quickly. How valuable is it, then, if we can contribute through KickstartAI and make our knowledge available?”

That is why he hopes more organizations will join. Not because every partner has to do the same thing, but because a larger network, in his view, makes more possible: more exchange, more learning capacity and ultimately more societal impact.

Dusée: “Those who work together not only increase the chances of scaling within their own organization, but also of creating real societal impact.”

--- Five lessons from Leon Dusée for other organizations ---

1. Choose sharply, and consciously leave things aside

According to Dusée, the biggest mistake is not experimenting too little, but wanting to do too much at once. Leaders must dare to say: this is where we will focus, and not there for now. Without focus, AI remains fragmented.

2. Look beyond the individual use case to the entire process

Many ideas rightly emerge bottom-up, but remain too small. The leadership question, he says, is always: can you broaden an application from one report or task to the entire process? That is where real scale lies.

3. Think about day two from day one

Pilots often stall because ownership, maintenance and embedding in existing processes are considered too late. Do not optimize for the demo, but for practice.

4. Invest as much in people as in technology

AI raises the demands placed on employees. Subject-matter expertise, adaptability and the ability to challenge output become more important, not less. Dusée also explicitly mentions learning on the job and a different perspective on education.

5. See AI as societal infrastructure too

According to Dusée, AI should not only be about productivity. Especially in sectors such as education, research and healthcare, collaboration can help make applications more accessible to organizations that do not themselves have the resources to lead the way.

💡 Want to learn more about the KickstartAI community and our mission to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands? Check out our website , listen to our podcast , or follow us on LinkedIn !

Thank you for subscribing to our newsletter!

source_quote: Without focus, AI remains fragmented.

source_text:
The questions Dusée encounters within ING are by no means unique to banks, he says. That is precisely why he is active within KickstartAI, the partnership involving organizations including KLM, NS, ING and Ahold Delhaize, which aims to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands. The sectors may differ, but the underlying AI challenges are often much more similar.

“KLM’s market is completely different from ING’s,” he says. “But you encounter the same challenges. From governance and data to the question: how do you move from a proof of concept to a real product?” For organizations, he believes, the value is immediate: learning faster what works and what does not.

For Dusée, KickstartAI also has a broader meaning than knowledge exchange between large companies. He explicitly sees it as a way to create more societal impact. As an example, he mentions an AI tool around financial health that ING is currently exploring  together with KickstartAI, with the aim of eventually investigating how customers can be helped earlier and more accessibly when financial problems are at risk of emerging.

Dusée: “How do you reach people earlier, lower barriers and offer help in a way that matches what people need? I find that at least as relevant. If we approach AI only as a machine for working more efficiently, we are selling ourselves short.”

But for Dusée, the value of KickstartAI extends beyond the partners alone. He sees the network not only as a place where large organizations exchange knowledge, but also as a way to make expertise more broadly available.

“There are so many organizations in the Netherlands, both profit and non-profit, where AI can be incredibly valuable,” he says. “In education, research, and healthcare, for example. But not everyone has the financial resources or capacity to apply AI quickly. How valuable is it, then, if we can contribute through KickstartAI and make our knowledge available?”

That is why he hopes more organizations will join. Not because every partner has to do the same thing, but because a larger network, in his view, makes more possible: more exchange, more learning capacity and ultimately more societal impact.

Dusée: “Those who work together not only increase the chances of scaling within their own organization, but also of creating real societal impact.”

--- Five lessons from Leon Dusée for other organizations ---

1. Choose sharply, and consciously leave things aside

According to Dusée, the biggest mistake is not experimenting too little, but wanting to do too much at once. Leaders must dare to say: this is where we will focus, and not there for now. Without focus, AI remains fragmented.

2. Look beyond the individual use case to the entire process

Many ideas rightly emerge bottom-up, but remain too small. The leadership question, he says, is always: can you broaden an application from one report or task to the entire process? That is where real scale lies.

3. Think about day two from day one

Pilots often stall because ownership, maintenance and embedding in existing processes are considered too late. Do not optimize for the demo, but for practice.

4. Invest as much in people as in technology

AI raises the demands placed on employees. Subject-matter expertise, adaptability and the ability to challenge output become more important, not less. Dusée also explicitly mentions learning on the job and a different perspective on education.

5. See AI as societal infrastructure too

According to Dusée, AI should not only be about productivity. Especially in sectors such as education, research and healthcare, collaboration can help make applications more accessible to organizations that do not themselves have the resources to lead the way.

💡 Want to learn more about the KickstartAI community and our mission to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands? Check out our website , listen to our podcast , or follow us on LinkedIn !

Thank you for subscribing to our newsletter!

belief: AI initiatives only create real value when users understand and trust the models.
source_quote: why an AI model only has value if users understand and trust it

====================================================================================================

--- AI lessons from the boardroom ---

=== KLM CIO Aart Slagt on why an AI model only has value if users understand and trust it ===

AI is now on almost every agenda. Not as a gadget, but as a strategic question: what should you use it for, how do you scale it, and how do you prevent experiments from getting stuck in the pilot phase? In the interview series "AI lessons from the boardroom," we speak with executives and board members who have to make those choices in practice. In this edition: Aart Slagt, Executive Vice President Information Services & CIO at KLM Royal Dutch Airlines. “The time for experimentation alone is over. We are now focused on truly implementing AI at the core of our business.”

Aart Slagt has already had a full day by the time he sits down to talk about artificial intelligence. Meetings, coordination, decisions, operational questions: as CIO of KLM, he constantly moves between technology and practice. That also fits his profile. Slagt is not an executive of fashionable buzzwords, but someone who comes from operations and still looks at things through that lens. After ten years in operational roles and now five years as CIO, he knows that plans fall apart if they do not resonate with colleagues on the operational side.

“We, too, have gone through a phase of experimentation here.” It’s been a useful stage, says Slagt, “because it helped the business and IT understand each other better.” But as far as he is concerned, that phase is now over. “We can no longer let a thousand flowers bloom. We want to create focus: where does KLM want to deploy AI to the fullest, which processes does the company want to redesign from an AI-first perspective, and where do we deliberately choose not to apply it?”

Not every part of KLM lends itself to far-reaching automation, Slagt knows. Some things you simply want people to do. “Take cabin service on our aircraft. That cannot exist without human contact.” But in other areas, AI can make a real difference, and that is exactly where you want to accelerate it: in planning, optimization, administration, and support. A good example is the model KLM built together with KickstartAI to better predict how much catering is needed on board , based on the actual number of passengers who end up taking the flight. It sounds small, but the impact is significant. “On the one hand, you can serve passengers better with the catering that is really needed,” says Slagt. “On the other hand, you prevent a huge amount of food waste, because far less has to be thrown away. So it is a double win.”

--- Trust and explainability ---

Another example Slagt is proud of concerns a joint project on contingency fuel : the additional fuel an aircraft carries for emergencies and unexpected circumstances. It naturally requires a delicate balance. Safety always comes first, but carrying too much fuel is also unsustainable and costly. “If you need it, it has to be on board. But you also do not want to take too much, because it adds unnecessary weight.”

Together with KickstartAI, KLM built a model that takes a higher number of variables into account than the traditional approach. Think of load, passenger numbers, wind, weather systems, and conditions at the destination. It is a unique model, but “ultimately, the captain is responsible and decides how much fuel is taken on board. So it is important that you can explain how such a model works, which variables go into it, and why the advice comes out the way it does.”

--- Knowledge ---

belief: The time for experimentation alone with AI is over and the focus must now be on implementing AI at the core of the business.
source_quote: The time for experimentation alone is over. We are now focused on truly implementing AI at the core of our business.

--- AI lessons from the boardroom ---

=== KLM CIO Aart Slagt on why an AI model only has value if users understand and trust it ===

AI is now on almost every agenda. Not as a gadget, but as a strategic question: what should you use it for, how do you scale it, and how do you prevent experiments from getting stuck in the pilot phase? In the interview series "AI lessons from the boardroom," we speak with executives and board members who have to make those choices in practice. In this edition: Aart Slagt, Executive Vice President Information Services & CIO at KLM Royal Dutch Airlines. “The time for experimentation alone is over. We are now focused on truly implementing AI at the core of our business.”

Aart Slagt has already had a full day by the time he sits down to talk about artificial intelligence. Meetings, coordination, decisions, operational questions: as CIO of KLM, he constantly moves between technology and practice. That also fits his profile. Slagt is not an executive of fashionable buzzwords, but someone who comes from operations and still looks at things through that lens. After ten years in operational roles and now five years as CIO, he knows that plans fall apart if they do not resonate with colleagues on the operational side.

“We, too, have gone through a phase of experimentation here.” It’s been a useful stage, says Slagt, “because it helped the business and IT understand each other better.” But as far as he is concerned, that phase is now over. “We can no longer let a thousand flowers bloom. We want to create focus: where does KLM want to deploy AI to the fullest, which processes does the company want to redesign from an AI-first perspective, and where do we deliberately choose not to apply it?”

Not every part of KLM lends itself to far-reaching automation, Slagt knows. Some things you simply want people to do. “Take cabin service on our aircraft. That cannot exist without human contact.” But in other areas, AI can make a real difference, and that is exactly where you want to accelerate it: in planning, optimization, administration, and support. A good example is the model KLM built together with KickstartAI to better predict how much catering is needed on board , based on the actual number of passengers who end up taking the flight. It sounds small, but the impact is significant. “On the one hand, you can serve passengers better with the catering that is really needed,” says Slagt. “On the other hand, you prevent a huge amount of food waste, because far less has to be thrown away. So it is a double win.”

--- Trust and explainability ---

Another example Slagt is proud of concerns a joint project on contingency fuel : the additional fuel an aircraft carries for emergencies and unexpected circumstances. It naturally requires a delicate balance. Safety always comes first, but carrying too much fuel is also unsustainable and costly. “If you need it, it has to be on board. But you also do not want to take too much, because it adds unnecessary weight.”

Together with KickstartAI, KLM built a model that takes a higher number of variables into account than the traditional approach. Think of load, passenger numbers, wind, weather systems, and conditions at the destination. It is a unique model, but “ultimately, the captain is responsible and decides how much fuel is taken on board. So it is important that you can explain how such a model works, which variables go into it, and why the advice comes out the way it does.”

--- Knowledge ---

belief: End users and executives must be closely and early involved in AI development and must understand both its capabilities and limitations for AI to create real change in the organisation.
source_quote: it is important for end users ... to be involved early in the development of such an application. And it is equally important for executives to understand both the capabilities and the limitations of AI.
source_text:
And that, according to Slagt, is exactly where things often go wrong. “A model may look excellent on paper, but if people do not understand it, nothing changes on the work floor.” That is precisely why it is important for end users (in this case pilots as well as the pilots’ union,) to be involved early in the development of such an application. And it is equally important for executives to understand both the capabilities and the limitations of AI. Slagt says: “If you have to make decisions about investments, risks, and applications, you cannot follow this from a distance. You really have to immerse yourself in what AI can and cannot do.”

At KLM, that meant the leadership team did not get yet another presentation, but instead had to sit down behind a laptop themselves, in this case at Google’s office. Slagt: “The assignment was simple: build your own AI assistant for an advertising campaign. Or create an agent that scans aviation news every week and puts it in your inbox. Only when you do it yourself do you see what AI can  deliver.”

KLM noticed the same thing when generative AI started advancing rapidly and employees wanted to start working with it. That is why KLM built Talia: a secure environment in which employees can safely work with models such as Gemini, Mistral, and ChatGPT. “It is important to keep building the bridge between the theory behind AI and its practical application. The colleagues that will work with it need to understand it, embrace it, and gain trust in it.”

--- The Dutch debate ---

But according to Slagt, the learning doesn't stop there. Anyone who really wants to make serious progress with AI also has to look outside their company.For that reason, KLM is active in KickstartAI, an initiative it co-founded with NS, Ahold, and ING. For Slagt, the value lies not only in the technology, but also in the way of collaborating: companies, sectors, and specialists coming together to solve real problems and put AI on the map in the Netherlands.

He calls KickstartAI, not without reason, “the coalition of the doing.” It is not a network of endless meetings and discussion groups, but a foundation that simply gets to work and delivers strong results.

And according to Slagt, that is exactly where the gain lies. “By joining forces with other companies, you bring in AI knowledge as well as  a fresh perspective on your own processes. It is precisely that combination of data and AI specialists with people who sometimes come from completely different sectors that produces the best solutions. I would like to see more companies in the Netherlands benefit from that by joining KickstartAI.”

It is a pragmatic and open perspective that is still far from self-evident in the Netherlands, Slagt observes. “ We sometimes focus too much on the harm AI could cause,” he says. “And then we surround the technology with fences, rules, and restrictions.”

A shame, Slagt believes. Because AI can make companies more productive, organize processes more intelligently, and strengthen the Netherlands as a knowledge economy. At the same time, international developments are not standing still. Other countries are investing heavily in AI and rapidly building up knowledge and applications. “If we do not do it, someone else will,” he concludes. “Then we will be left behind. It would be a great shame if we let that happen.”

--- Three lessons from Aart Slagt for other organizations ---

1. Stop open-ended experimentation in time Pilots and proof-of-concepts are necessary to explore possibilities and bring business and IT closer together. But at a certain point, an organization has to make choices. Where do you really want to deploy AI, what do you want to scale up, and where do you deliberately choose not to apply the technology?

source_quote: It is important to keep building the bridge between the theory behind AI and its practical application.

Other countries are investing heavily in AI and rapidly building up knowledge and applications. “If we do not do it, someone else will,” he concludes. “Then we will be left behind. It would be a great shame if we let that happen.”

--- Three lessons from Aart Slagt for other organizations ---

1. Stop open-ended experimentation in time Pilots and proof-of-concepts are necessary to explore possibilities and bring business and IT closer together. But at a certain point, an organization has to make choices. Where do you really want to deploy AI, what do you want to scale up, and where do you deliberately choose not to apply the technology?

belief: Hands-on experience with AI by both executives and employees is a necessary condition for successful organizational adoption of the technology.
source_quote: Only when you do it yourself do you see what AI can  deliver.
inference_reasoning: The text contrasts passive presentations with active building of AI assistants and links understanding, trust, and change on the work floor to direct use, implying that practical, first-hand engagement is strategically required for effective adoption.
source_text:
And that, according to Slagt, is exactly where things often go wrong. “A model may look excellent on paper, but if people do not understand it, nothing changes on the work floor.” That is precisely why it is important for end users (in this case pilots as well as the pilots’ union,) to be involved early in the development of such an application. And it is equally important for executives to understand both the capabilities and the limitations of AI. Slagt says: “If you have to make decisions about investments, risks, and applications, you cannot follow this from a distance. You really have to immerse yourself in what AI can and cannot do.”

At KLM, that meant the leadership team did not get yet another presentation, but instead had to sit down behind a laptop themselves, in this case at Google’s office. Slagt: “The assignment was simple: build your own AI assistant for an advertising campaign. Or create an agent that scans aviation news every week and puts it in your inbox. Only when you do it yourself do you see what AI can  deliver.”

KLM noticed the same thing when generative AI started advancing rapidly and employees wanted to start working with it. That is why KLM built Talia: a secure environment in which employees can safely work with models such as Gemini, Mistral, and ChatGPT. “It is important to keep building the bridge between the theory behind AI and its practical application. The colleagues that will work with it need to understand it, embrace it, and gain trust in it.”

--- The Dutch debate ---

But according to Slagt, the learning doesn't stop there. Anyone who really wants to make serious progress with AI also has to look outside their company.For that reason, KLM is active in KickstartAI, an initiative it co-founded with NS, Ahold, and ING. For Slagt, the value lies not only in the technology, but also in the way of collaborating: companies, sectors, and specialists coming together to solve real problems and put AI on the map in the Netherlands.

He calls KickstartAI, not without reason, “the coalition of the doing.” It is not a network of endless meetings and discussion groups, but a foundation that simply gets to work and delivers strong results.

And according to Slagt, that is exactly where the gain lies. “By joining forces with other companies, you bring in AI knowledge as well as  a fresh perspective on your own processes. It is precisely that combination of data and AI specialists with people who sometimes come from completely different sectors that produces the best solutions. I would like to see more companies in the Netherlands benefit from that by joining KickstartAI.”

It is a pragmatic and open perspective that is still far from self-evident in the Netherlands, Slagt observes. “ We sometimes focus too much on the harm AI could cause,” he says. “And then we surround the technology with fences, rules, and restrictions.”

A shame, Slagt believes. Because AI can make companies more productive, organize processes more intelligently, and strengthen the Netherlands as a knowledge economy. At the same time, international developments are not standing still. Other countries are investing heavily in AI and rapidly building up knowledge and applications. “If we do not do it, someone else will,” he concludes. “Then we will be left behind. It would be a great shame if we let that happen.”

--- Three lessons from Aart Slagt for other organizations ---

1. Stop open-ended experimentation in time Pilots and proof-of-concepts are necessary to explore possibilities and bring business and IT closer together. But at a certain point, an organization has to make choices. Where do you really want to deploy AI, what do you want to scale up, and where do you deliberately choose not to apply the technology?

belief: AI applications should be built for users so that they understand and trust them, rather than being built just for the technology itself.
source_quote: Do not build a model for the technology, build it for the user
source_text:
2. Do not build a model for the technology, build it for the user An application only creates value if the people who have to work with it understand it and trust it. Especially in regulated environments, AI must be explainable, align with professional responsibility, and be developed together with end users. That is why, for example, KLM involved pilots at a very early stage in the development process of its contingency fuel application.

3. Make AI a board-level issue, not a specialist silo Invest in the knowledge level of the company’s leadership. Anyone who wants to steer investments, risks, and implementation must personally understand what the technology can and cannot do. That requires not only vision, but also hands-on experience.

💡 Want to learn more about the KickstartAI community and our mission to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands? Check out our website , listen to our podcast , or follow us on LinkedIn !

Thank you for subscribing to our newsletter!

belief: Successful AI adoption requires focus, clear choices, and early thinking about how solutions will scale within the organization.
source_quote: successful AI adoption requires focus, clear choices, and early thinking about how solutions will scale within the organization
source_text:
AI is high on every agenda. But for many organizations, the question is where to start – and how to move beyond isolated initiatives.

In our latest article, we speak with Leon Dusée, Global Head COO Risk at ING about how to apply AI in practice. He explains that successful AI adoption requires focus, clear choices, and early thinking about how solutions will scale within the organization.

In the interview, Dusée shares lessons on:
🔸 Why AI pilots often stall
🔸 What leaders need to take into account when implementing AI solutions
🔸 The importance of investing in people alongside technology
🔸 And how AI can contribute to broader societal impact

belief: Enterprise AI development requires specialized tools, infrastructure, and security strategies beyond generic AI practices.
source_quote: focused on the tools, infrastructure, and strategies necessary to build secure, full-stack AI applications for the enterprise
inference_reasoning: By emphasizing what is "necessary" specifically for enterprise AI and highlighting security and full-stack concerns, the text implies that enterprise AI has distinct, more demanding requirements than general AI work.
source_text:
Last night, KickstartAI teamed up with Cisco to host an inspiring community meetup focused on the tools, infrastructure, and strategies necessary to build secure, full-stack AI applications for the enterprise.

🌟 Quick recap: 

🔧 Boris Vermaas broke down Cisco's concept of the Secure AI Factory and how to think about the foundations needed to run AI applications securely at enterprise level. 

⚙️ Sebastiaan van Duijn shared practical lessons from building an agentic AI system for Trivium Packaging, using the infrastructure and Secure AI Factory concept from Cisco and NVIDIA.

🛠️ Igor Soltysik walked us through highlights from NVIDIA GTC, including NIM APIs and NemoClaw – a new open-source stack designed to make AI agents safer to run in enterprise environments.

A big thanks to the speakers who shared their knowledge, and to everyone who joined the event! 

Stay tuned for our upcoming meetup in May 🗓️ 
#EnterpriseAI #AICommunity #AIMeetup #Nvidia #KickstartAI #Cisco

inference_reasoning: The positive framing of the meetup and gratitude to speakers and attendees, combined with a promise of future events, implies a belief that such knowledge-sharing gatherings are a useful strategy to move enterprise AI forward.

belief: There is an urgent need to move from talking about AI to executing and applying it in practice.
source_quote: the urgency is there – now execution needs to follow
source_text:
Last Thursday, our CEO Erick Webbe joined the roundtable discussion in the Dutch Parliament (Tweede Kamer der Staten-Generaal) on how the Netherlands can make better use of the opportunities AI offers. 🇳🇱 

Our view is clear: the urgency is there – now execution needs to follow.
As Erick put it: "We need to take AI out of the ivory tower and into practice.”

That broader message was reflected in several parts of the discussion. As Maarten de Rijke also noted, the Netherlands has spent a long time thinking through the rules around AI; now it is time to start playing.

The Netherlands already has a strong foundation – from long-term research through ELLIS - European Laboratory for Learning and Intelligent Systems, to mid-term collaboration through ICAI - Innovation Center for Artificial Intelligence labs, to short-cycle implementation through initiatives like KickstartAI. What is still missing is not ideas, talent, or infrastructure, but turning that capacity into actual use.

The next step is to build on what already works. As @Petra Piersma noted, that means focusing on scaling proven solutions. 📊 

That’s the part we focus on at KickstartAI: working with organisations to apply AI in practice and sharing the learnings so they can be replicated across the Dutch ecosystem. 

We’d be happy to welcome interested MPs Claire Martens - America, Henk Vermeer, Ouafa Oualhadj, Jantine Zwinkels, Sarah El Boujdaini, Barbara Kathmann to one of our upcoming project launches and show what that real life AI solutions look like in action. 🚀  

#VVD #CDA #BBB #D66 #PRO #KickstartAI #AI

belief: AI should genuinely serve the people who end up using it.
source_quote: Making sure AI genuinely serves the people who end up using it.
source_text:
🚀 Meet Anouk Wolters, AI Project Lead at KickstartAI!

Anouk works at the intersection of technology, people, and implementation – helping turn AI ideas into concrete solutions.

What drives her the most? Making sure AI genuinely serves the people who end up using it. belief: Organisations should not have to work through new AI developments in isolation but instead build on each other’s experiences.
source_quote: so organisations don't have to work through these developments in isolation, but can build on each other’s experiences
source_text:
🚀 This month, we kicked off the first of our new Knowledge Sharing Sessions with our corporate partners: Ahold Delhaize, ING, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines, and Nederlandse Spoorwegen.

These closed-door sessions are designed to create space to share what teams are seeing, testing, and learning – so organisations don't have to work through these developments in isolation, but can build on each other’s experiences.

For this first edition, we focused on MCPs (Model Context Protocol) and AI agents, opening with a keynote by Bob van Luijt. We moved into roundtable discussions on where these building blocks are already showing up, what teams are experimenting with, and where the practical questions still are.

The goal is simple: by turning these insights into a shared knowledge base, the whole group can move forward faster. A big thank you to all the participants! 👏 

#AIAgents #MCPs #KickstartAI #AppliedAI

belief: The Netherlands has many prerequisites in place for AI, but is losing momentum due to low AI implementation capacity and outdated structures.
source_quote: our position is clear: The Netherlands 🇳🇱  has many prerequisites in place – talent, infrastructure, and a strong business climate – but we are losing momentum to low AI implementation capacity and structures that weren't built for the speed of this technology.
source_text:
We are proud to share that our CEO, Erick Webbe, will be taking part in a roundtable discussion at the Dutch Parliament (Tweede Kamer der Staten-Generaal) on 26 March about optimizing AI opportunities for the Netherlands.

At KickstartAI, our position is clear: The Netherlands 🇳🇱  has many prerequisites in place – talent, infrastructure, and a strong business climate – but we are losing momentum to low AI implementation capacity and structures that weren't built for the speed of this technology. As a result, we're not realizing the full potential of AI and its benefits for society.

Erick will be advocating for a national shift from talk to action. To do that, we need:
✅ Space for execution and a mandate for practical experimentation. 
✅ Moving past existing structures to prioritize performance and scale. 
✅ Bridging the impact gap by tackling today’s most complex challenges

We know this is possible through our own projects – from reducing food waste with Ahold Delhaize, to optimizing logistics with Ampère, and improving financial health with ING.

📖 You can find our full position paper on the Parliament’s website here: https://lnkd.in/e2X6exwK 

Looking forward to the conversation Claire Martens - America, Judith Bühler, Ouafa Oualhadj, Barbara Kathmann, Tom van der Lee, Daniël van den Berg!

belief: Starting AI implementation with the technology instead of the problem is a common trap that organizations should avoid.
source_quote: a common trap in AI implementation: starting with the technology instead of the problem
source_text:
In an interview published this week, Erick Webbe, our CEO, points out a common trap in AI implementation: starting with the technology instead of the problem.

“We want to do something with AI” is still where many organizations begin. But without a clear problem to solve, that’s usually where things start to stall.

As AI becomes a “system technology”– much like electricity or the internet – what does deliberate application look like and what kind of challenges do organizations need to solve?

👉 Read the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/ewkX_w6Z

source_text:
Don't miss our April meetup in Eindhoven! Together with Cisco, we’ve created an evening focused on how to build secure, full-stack AI applications that can scale in enterprise environments.

On the agenda: 
📊 Boris Vermaas (AI Infrastructure,  Cisco) will introduce the concept of a Secure AI Factory for designing scalable, production-ready AI infrastructure.

🔧 Sebastiaan van Duijn (AI CoE Leader,Trivium Packaging) will sharing a real-world case study on building a custom AI application on the Cisco stack and the journey of scaling it.

⚙️ Igor Soltysik (Channel Solutions Architect Benelux & Nordics, NVIDIA) will provide a deep dive into building and deploying applications on NVIDIA AI, including the latest technical developments and hardware updates from NVIDIA GTC.

🥂 Networking drinks and bites

Spots are limited, RSVP using the link below 👇 

#KickstartAIMeetup #AIInfrastructure #EnterpriseAI

belief: AI is particularly well-suited to help manage the complexity of legal and investigative work in environmental crime cases.
source_quote: an AI tool that helps investigators navigate legislation, case law and similar cases more quickly
inference_reasoning: By proposing AI specifically to handle complex legislation and case materials, the organisation implies a belief that AI is an appropriate and effective technology for this type of cognitively complex legal navigation.
source_text:
Environmental crime investigations are some of the most complex cases police deal with, multiple layers of legislation.

This is why, together with Politie Nederland, KickstartAI is working on an AI tool that helps investigators navigate legislation, case law and similar cases more quickly, supporting the early analysis phase of environmental crime investigations.

We spoke with Amir Niknam, senior innovation advisor at the Police, about the project and the role AI can play in supporting investigators. 

🎓 His interview also kicks off our new series "AI Lessons from the Boardroom", where we speak with executives about how they approach the governance and use of AI in their organizations.

👇 Read the full article below: 
https://lnkd.in/euaR2tME

#AI #EnvironmentalCrime #AILessonsFromTheBoardroom

belief: AI products should be designed starting from human psychology, workflows, and trust.
source_quote: Start with human psychology, workflows, and trust.
source_text:
🚀 LastFriday, we had a full house at our KickstartAI x Data Science Rotterdam meetup on scaling AI products.

We heard valuable insights from founders navigating this journey every day:
🔸 Leen de Graaf (Cuurios) on building targeted AI solutions instead of one-size-fits-all products
 🔸 Guus van Heijningen (Captain) on raising funding for an AI startup
 🔸 Sohrab Hosseini (orq.ai) and Karthik Mahadevan (Envision) on the realities of scaling 

💡 Key takeaways:
 • Start with human psychology, workflows, and trust. 
 • Think like an investor when building your pitch.
 • Build internationally from day one.

Thanks to our speakers, the evening's moderator Michael Niemantsverdriet, and everyone who joined the discussion.

Save the date for our upcoming meetup on April 2!
#AIMeetup  #ScalingAI

belief: AI should be more than a technological upgrade.
source_quote: AI should be more than a technological upgrade
source_text:
We’re proud that KickstartAI was shortlisted as a finalist for the Nederlandse AI-Award this week, as one of the initiatives contributing to the Dutch AI ecosystem 🇳🇱

For us, the nomination reflects a year of work with partners, collaborators, and our broader community, with a focus on applying AI to the most pressing challenges. 

Congratulations to the winner GPT-NL and to the fellow finalists The European Trust Alliance, vlam.ai by SSC-ICT, and De Nederlandse AI Fabriek 👏

Glad to be part of an ecosystem pushing responsible AI forward in the Netherlands. We believe AI should be more than a technological upgrade. Used well, it can become a societal advantage. 

#NederlandseAIAward #ΑΙ #ΑΙNetherlands

belief: Data composition and data quality are just as crucial to progress in language models as bigger architectures and larger datasets.
source_quote: data composition and data quality are just as crucial
source_text:
We often credit progress in language models to bigger architectures and ever-larger datasets. But questions of data composition and data quality are just as crucial – and far less understood.

📖 In our latest article, we spoke with Dong Nguyen (Associate Professor at Utrecht University) about DataDivers, an ongoing research project on how to measure data diversity in language model datasets – and how this can help us better understand model behavior and performance.

Read the full article below 👇 
https://lnkd.in/e9CD-i8j

#AI #DataDiversity #LLMs #NLP

belief: The application of AI in the Netherlands requires a sense of urgency and implementation of the coalition agreement commitments.
source_quote: Het toepassen van AI in Nederland vraagt om urgentie én om het uitvoeren van de afspraken uit het coalitieakkoord.
source_text:
Hartelijk dank voor jullie bezoek, Ouafa Oualhadj, Sarah El Boujdaini en Fabian Snijders (D66), aan KickstartAI. Waardevol om – juist op de dag dat Jetten I van start ging – samen te brainstormen over hoe we concrete stappen kunnen zetten in AI-adoptie.

💡 Op de agenda:
 🔸 Welke lessen kunnen we leren uit de praktijk van AI-adoptie? Denk aan het oplossen van maatschappelijke vraagstukken, het effectiever maken van de overheid en het versterken van bedrijven met AI.
 🔸 Concrete voorbeelden: we deelden hoe AI nu al aantoonbare waarde toevoegt voor onze partners en de organisaties waarmee we samenwerken.
 🔸 Verantwoorde AI: een open en eerlijke dialoog over wat nodig is voor veilige, ethische én innovatieve AI-toepassingen in Nederland.

Het toepassen van AI in Nederland vraagt om urgentie én om het uitvoeren van de afspraken uit het coalitieakkoord. Nu is het moment om woorden om te zetten in daden. Dat vraagt om durf om actie te nemen – bijvoorbeeld via sterke publiek-private samenwerking.

Veel dank voor jullie komst, jullie betrokkenheid bij AI en – bovenal – de gedeelde ambitie om de kansen van AI in de praktijk te realiseren! 

#AIAdoptie #Innovatie #DigitaleZaken

belief: Being recognised alongside other notable AI initiatives enhances KickstartAI’s legitimacy and standing within the Dutch AI ecosystem.
source_quote: an honour to stand alongside other impactful initiatives and organizations shaping the Netherland's AI landscape
inference_reasoning: Describing it as an honour to be listed with other initiatives implies that such association is seen as reputationally valuable and legitimising.
source_text:
We’re proud to share that KickstartAI has been nominated for the "Nederlandse AI-Award" as one of the selected initiatives contributing to the Dutch AI ecosystem! 🇳🇱

This nomination is a reflection of the collective work of our team, our partners, our community, and supporters who are accelerating responsible AI adoption and creating real societal impact.

It's also an honour to stand alongside other impactful initiatives and organizations shaping the Netherland's AI landscape: GPT-NL, The European Trust Alliance, vlam.ai by SSC-ICT, and De Nederlandse AI Fabriek. 

We look forward to the ceremony on March 4 🚀  A special thanks to Jim Stolze for bringing these awards to life!

belief: People considering a career in AI should focus on understanding the fundamentals rather than getting attached to specific tools or techniques.
source_quote: “Don’t get too attached to specific tools or techniques. Focus on understanding the fundamentals – they’ll always stay relevant.”
source_text:
🚀Meet Jorrit van der Laan, ML Engineer at KickstartAI!

With a background in mathematics and data science, Jorrit enjoys solving problems and finding patterns.

What he finds special about the AI field is that deep technical work goes hand in hand with collaborating with people from diverse backgrounds and understanding different perspectives.

💡His advice to people considering a career in AI? “Don’t get too attached to specific tools or techniques. Focus on understanding the fundamentals – they’ll always stay relevant.”

Read his full story in the comments 👇

#AI #MachineLearning #AIEngineers #KickstartAI

source_quote: how to navigate all the hurdles in between

source_text:
Join us for a KickstartAI x Data Science Rotterdam meetup on scaling AI products – from prototype to market – and how to navigate all the hurdles in between. 

You'll hear from: 

⚙️ Leen de Graaf (CEO, Cuurios) on building targeted AI solutions instead of rigid “one-size-fits-all” products

💰 Guus van Heijningen (Co-founder, Captain) on lessons from fundraising for an AI startup 

📊 Sohrab Hosseini (Co-founder, orq.ai) & Karthik Mahadevan (CEO, Envision) in a fireside chat on what “market-ready” AI means today

PS: Drinks & dinner on us! RSVP below👇 

#AI #Scaling #AIStartups #AICommunity #KickstartAICommunity

belief: AI can help society thrive for the benefit of all.
source_quote: a shared belief that AI can help our society thrive for the benefit of all
source_text:
It was a real pleasure being part of last week’s AI4ALL Gala & Awards – an evening of inspiration, celebration, and a shared belief that AI can help our society thrive for the benefit of all.

We were honoured to sponsor the AI4Her Earth Award, recognizing the use of AI to address environmental challenges, and proud to have our CEO Erick Webbe present it to Dr. Rimma Dzhusupova, whose work applies AI to optimize resource-intensive industries – from improving complex engineering processes to supporting offshore green hydrogen production.

Congratulations to Rimma on this well-deserved recognition, to fellow category finalists Anna Brueva and Inge van den Ende, and to all the nominees and winners of the evening. ✨ 

Events like this show why community matters.

If AI is to strengthen our economy and society we have to keep building ecosystems where people collaborate, share knowledge, and turn ideas into action. 

👏 A big thank you to the AI4ALL founders Oliviana Bailey, Karen S. Harriss, MBA, GMA, MA, and Jana Wilhelmus for all their work! 

📸 Credits to Lera Manzovitova for the first two photos. 

#AI #KickstartAI #AI4ALL

belief: Sharing knowledge, building collectively, and accelerating AI adoption across the Netherlands are important goals for the organisation and its partners.
source_quote: we believe in sharing knowledge, building collectively, and accelerating AI adoption across the Netherlands
source_text:
Exciting news! We’ve completed the KickstartAI x bol Challenge: Optimizing the Delivery Promise 🚚

Last month, 16 engineers from KickstartAI, bol, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines, ING, Ampère, Nederlandse Spoorwegen, and Ahold Delhaize came together for a two-week AI sprint.

💡 The focus was practical: working on a delivery prediction model with the ultimate goal to improve customer experience on bol’s platform. 

Working in mixed teams, participants tested ideas, compared approaches, and built on each other’s work in a short, hands-on setup. A visit to a bol warehouse added valuable context and helped connect the technical work to day-to-day operations. 

The challenge concluded with a closing session and drinks, wrapping up two weeks of close collaboration across organizations. Together with our partners, we believe in sharing knowledge, building collectively, and accelerating AI adoption across the Netherlands 🇳🇱

#AI #MachineLearning #AIinNL #Collaboration

belief: Cross-organizational collaboration is more effective for advancing AI solutions than working within a single company.
source_quote: close collaboration across organizations
inference_reasoning: By emphasizing mixed teams from multiple companies and highlighting cross-organizational collaboration as a key feature of the challenge, the text implies that such collaboration is strategically superior for developing AI solutions.

source_quote: short, hands-on setup
inference_reasoning: The description of a short, hands-on setup and a warehouse visit to connect technical work to operations implies a belief that practical, context-embedded experimentation is the best way to build useful AI models.
source_text:
Exciting news! We’ve completed the KickstartAI x bol Challenge: Optimizing the Delivery Promise 🚚

Last month, 16 engineers from KickstartAI, bol, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines, ING, Ampère, Nederlandse Spoorwegen, and Ahold Delhaize came together for a two-week AI sprint.

💡 The focus was practical: working on a delivery prediction model with the ultimate goal to improve customer experience on bol’s platform. 

Working in mixed teams, participants tested ideas, compared approaches, and built on each other’s work in a short, hands-on setup. A visit to a bol warehouse added valuable context and helped connect the technical work to day-to-day operations. 

The challenge concluded with a closing session and drinks, wrapping up two weeks of close collaboration across organizations. Together with our partners, we believe in sharing knowledge, building collectively, and accelerating AI adoption across the Netherlands 🇳🇱

#AI #MachineLearning #AIinNL #Collaboration

belief: The application of AI in the Netherlands requires taking concrete steps, allowing room to experiment, and strong public–private collaboration.
source_quote: Het toepassen van AI in Nederland vraagt om concrete stappen zetten, ruimte om te experimenteren en sterke publiek-private samenwerking.
source_text:
Inspirerend bezoek van CDA-Kamerlid Jantine Zwinkels aan KickstartAI
in Delft! Tijdens haar werkbezoek sloegen we de brug tussen de Haagse politiek en de praktijk van AI-adoptie.

💡Wat  kwam aan bod? 

🔸 Praktijk vs. Politiek: Hoe vertalen we lessen uit de tech-sector naar effectief beleid?
🔸 Casussen in beeld: Samen met onze partners lieten we zien hoe AI nu al concrete waarde toevoegt aan de Nederlandse economie.
🔸 Verantwoorde AI: Een open dialoog over wat er nodig is voor veilige, ethische en innovatieve AI-toepassingen in Nederland.

Het toepassen van AI in Nederland vraagt om concrete stappen zetten, ruimte om te experimenteren en sterke publiek-private samenwerking. Dank Jantine voor je scherpe blik en betrokkenheid bij de digitale toekomst van Nederland!

belief: The urgency around labour productivity, national earning capacity, and ensuring a positive societal impact from AI is undeniable and demands immediate action.
source_quote: The urgency around labour productivity, our national earning capacity, and ensuring a positive societal impact  is undeniable – and it demands action now.
source_text:
On Monday, KickstartAI joined Minister Vincent Karremans and a small group of industry leaders at the Ministry of Economic Affairs for a roundtable on AI adoption in the Netherlands.

🇳🇱 The urgency around labour productivity, our national earning capacity, and ensuring a positive societal impact  is undeniable – and it demands action now. If we want to stay competitive, we need to treat the Netherlands as a scale-up country for AI adoption – moving beyond high-level reflections to the reality of implementation in day-to-day operations.

For the past four years, KickstartAI and our founding partners have been working exactly there: in the core of organizations. Our experience shows that meaningful AI adoption is a steep learning curve. It means tackling real problems, embedding AI into core processes, and dealing with culture and governance – not just rolling out tools.

We believe large companies play a crucial role as the flywheel for Dutch AI innovation and practical adoption at scale. Our new CEO Erick Webbe indicated we are committed to staying the course, with the ambition to build on it nationally and deliver a tenfold increase in lasting impact: with big and small businesses. 

Looking at the new coalition agreement this is a shared goal. The focus now must be on turning it into practice. 

A sincere thank you to Vincent Karremans and Martijn Lucassen for the invitation and to all participants for the open and practical discussion Art de Blaauw, Hiek Van der Scheer, Juliette Goetzee, Saskia Mureau, Thomas Grosfeld, Jelle Prins!

belief: Organisations that believe in the economy of the future and want to be digital frontrunners must take concrete steps in AI.
source_quote: Wie gelooft in de economie van de toekomst en digitaal koploper wil zijn, zal hier echt stappen in moeten zetten.
source_text:
De ambitie op AI is duidelijk verankerd in het coalitieakkoord ‘Aan de Slag.’ Mooi om te zien dat dit wordt vertaald naar concrete acties: inzet op een NADI, aandacht voor AI-geletterdheid, aanpassing van de WBSO om de ontwikkeling van AI te stimuleren, en uiteraard een verantwoorde inzet van AI binnen de overheid zelf. 

Wie gelooft in de economie van de toekomst en digitaal koploper wil zijn, zal hier echt stappen in moeten zetten.

➡️ Van pilot naar implementatie; van klein beginnen naar opschalen en vooral in de praktijk doen. Bij KickstartAI herkennen we dat: al meer dan vijf jaar werken wij aan het versnellen van AI-adoptie. Wij kijken ernaar uit deze uitdaging samen met Kabinet en Kamer op te pakken.

belief: Responsible and scalable use of generative AI is particularly important when dealing with people’s financial health.
source_quote: support financial health in a responsible and scalable way
inference_reasoning: By explicitly pairing financial health with the need for responsibility and scalability, the text implies that these qualities are especially critical in this domain, not just nice-to-have features.
source_text:
How do you build and evaluate a generative AI chatbot for financial health? 💬 

In a new technical blog, Julian von der Goltz, ML Engineer at KickstartAI, shares insights from our recent challenge with ING – where participants from Ahold Delhaize, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines, Nederlandse Spoorwegen, bol, ING, and KickstartAI joined forces to explore how an GenAI-powered chatbot could support financial health in a responsible and scalable way.

The blog highlights how the team: 
🔸 set up the challenge 
🔸 evaluated the chatbot using synthetic conversations, LLM-based judges, and deterministic metrics
🔸 customized the chatbot to adapt to the tone and style of different users 
🔸 combined a multi-agent architecture, retrieval, tool calling, and guardrails to keep responses accurate and within scope

👇 Read the full article below
https://lnkd.in/dGZ5dEHg

#GenAI #FinancialHealth #Chatbot

belief: Transparency and alignment with local cultural and regulatory values are important qualities for large language models used in Europe.
source_quote: a transparent, Dutch-language foundation model aligned with European values
inference_reasoning: By highlighting transparency and alignment with European values as defining features of the showcased model, the text implies these are desirable and important qualities for LLMs in this context.
source_text:
Yesterday, we held our first KickstartAI community technical meetup of the year, joining forces with PyData Amsterdam to dive into the practical realities of engineering LLMs. We dove into: 

🇳🇱 Training Sovereign Models from Scratch:
Julio Alexandrino Oliveira Filho and Alexandru Turcu took us behind the scenes of GPT-NL, breaking down the immense engineering effort required to build a transparent, Dutch-language foundation model aligned with European values. 

🔒 Architecting Privacy-First Environments:
Eduardo Bonet shared lessons from GitLab on “Bring Your Own Model” workflows. His talk focused on the architecture of GitLab Duo Self-Hosted, showing how to design model-agnostic systems that enable high-performance AI while maintaining strict governance and privacy guarantees.

A big thank you to everyone for being here and to the House of Watt for hosting us! 🌟 

#AI #KickstartAImeetup #PyDataAmsterdam #LLMs

belief: Strong privacy and governance safeguards are essential requirements for deploying high‑performance AI systems in organisations.

inference_reasoning: The contrastive framing of performance alongside governance and privacy implies that robust safeguards are not optional but necessary when architecting AI systems.

We discuss:
🔸 Why dashboards alone often fall short once questions start piling up
🔸 How to integrate agents into real data architectures
🔸 How to approach trust, transparency, and safety

belief: Progress in AI is not just about technical capability but about making deliberate choices along the way.
source_quote: progress isn’t just about technical capability, but about making deliberate choices along the way
source_text:
Last Friday, the KickstartAI community gathered at the Hortus Botanicus Amsterdam to start 2026 by (re)connecting with each other and discussing our work in AI 🤝 

Through facilitated networking rounds, people quickly got specific about their roles, current challenges, and what kind of collaboration would actually be useful. 

From there, Roel Willems (Ahold Delhaize) offered an update on the state of AI adoption in the Netherlands – a timely reminder that progress isn’t just about technical capability, but about making deliberate choices along the way.

We ended with a guided walk through the gardens, which turned out to be a fitting metaphor: healthy ecosystems depend on diversity, cross-pollination, and long-term thinking – much like the AI ecosystem we’re building. 

🌟 A big thank you to everyone who joined! 

#KickstartAICommunity #AINetherlands #AI

Through facilitated networking rounds, people quickly got specific about their roles, current challenges, and what kind of collaboration would actually be useful. 

From there, Roel Willems (Ahold Delhaize) offered an update on the state of AI adoption in the Netherlands – a timely reminder that progress isn’t just about technical capability, but about making deliberate choices along the way.

We ended with a guided walk through the gardens, which turned out to be a fitting metaphor: healthy ecosystems depend on diversity, cross-pollination, and long-term thinking – much like the AI ecosystem we’re building. 

🌟 A big thank you to everyone who joined! 

#KickstartAICommunity #AINetherlands #AI

belief: A robust national AI ecosystem in the Netherlands depends on deliberate, long-term, and diverse collaboration rather than purely short-term technical gains.

inference_reasoning: The ecosystem metaphor and comparison to their AI efforts imply a belief that sustainable AI development in the Netherlands must prioritise diversity, cross-pollination, and long-termism over narrow or short-term technical focus.

belief: Understanding how AI and Earth observation are changing decision-making is important for practitioners and stakeholders in related fields.
source_quote: How are AI and Earth observation changing decision-making on the ground?
inference_reasoning: By opening with a question about how AI and EO are changing decision-making and then promoting an article that explores this, the organisation implies that grasping these changes is a meaningful and worthwhile concern for its audience.
source_text:
🌍 How are AI and Earth observation changing decision-making on the ground?

Public space data, commercial satellite imagery, and recent advances in AI are expanding what’s possible with Earth observation – both in how data is accessed and how it’s analysed.

In our latest article, we look at current and evolving AI uses in EO and examine different EO markets and applications, from climate monitoring and biodiversity assessment to regional policy-making and emerging commercial use cases. 

We spoke with Parya Pasha Zadeh (Program Manager at NL Space Campus) and Thijs Oosterhuis (Solutions Architect for Geo and Remote Sensing at @ Provincie Zuid-Holland) about how these developments are unfolding in practice, drawing on perspectives from applied EO work in the Netherlands.

👇 Read the full article below:
https://lnkd.in/esPQitmu

#AI #EarthObservation #GIS

belief: Cross-sector, practice-based perspectives are valuable for making sense of developments in AI and Earth observation.

inference_reasoning: Highlighting that the article draws on applied EO work and named practitioners suggests the organisation values grounded, real-world perspectives rather than purely theoretical discussion.

belief: Forming coalitions among European countries is an effective way to accelerate progress on strategic initiatives.
source_quote: identifying coalitions that could move faster on strategic initiatives
inference_reasoning: The text frames coalitions as a means to "move faster" on strategic initiatives, implying a belief that coalition-building is a key strategic lever for accelerating action.
source_text:
Over the past months, we collaborated with DenkWerk on their new report examining Europe’s geopolitical and geo-economic position – and what it will take to strengthen Europe’s ability to act.

💡Thanks to the work of our ML Engineer Cascha van Wanrooij we used AI to transcribe and analyze public sessions of the Council of the EU to extract how countries position themselves on different topics and where their views align.

This made it possible to create cluster maps showing potential areas for collaboration – a key starting point for identifying coalitions that could move faster on strategic initiatives such as shared European infrastructure.

👉Read the full report “Market and Power: Player or Playing Field in the Hegemony Struggle: https://lnkd.in/enPriPN4

belief: AI-based analysis of political proceedings is a valuable tool for understanding and improving European geopolitical decision-making.

inference_reasoning: By highlighting the AI analysis as enabling insights into country positions and collaboration opportunities, the text implies a positive stance on AI as a useful and legitimate tool in geopolitical analysis and policymaking contexts.

belief: Divergent expert perspectives on AI progress and AGI are important and worth examining in depth.
source_quote: reflect on why leading AI thinkers interpret last year's developments so differently
inference_reasoning: By centering the podcast on explaining why experts disagree, the organisation implies that understanding these divergences is valuable and deserving of focused reflection.
source_text:
What did the great minds in AI say about 2025’s progress – and what do they predict for the future?

🎙️ In our latest podcast, Cascha van Wanrooij, Evertjan Peer, and Nic van Vliet reflect on why leading AI thinkers interpret last year's developments so differently: from post-scaling researchers like Ilya Sutskever calling for new ideas, to fundamental sceptics such as Richard Sutton questioning today's dominant approaches, and to lab leaders like Demis Hassabis continuing to see scaling as the main driver of progress. 

How do these differences carry over to views on AGI?

🎧 Listen to the full conversation here: https://lnkd.in/e9TTVz9G

#AI #AGI #AIResearch #TheKickstartAIPodcast

belief: Economic shocks in an interconnected economy rarely occur in isolation and can reinforce each other into broader crises.
source_quote: In an interconnected economy, shocks rarely occur in isolation and can reinforce each other, escalating into broader crises.
source_text:
In an interconnected economy, shocks rarely occur in isolation and can reinforce each other, escalating into broader crises.

In this ESB article below, our ML engineer Cascha van Wanrooij, together with Barbara Baarsma and Ruth Smalbraak, analyze what this means for economic risks and resilience in the Netherlands. The article draws on a meta-analysis of more than 5,000 policy documents, using AI to identify and map interdependencies, as part of DenkWerk’s 2025 report: “Resilience by design – the five principles for a resilient Netherlands.”

👉 Link to the full analysis: https://lnkd.in/ejk3h7F7

belief: KickstartAI aims to push AI forward by building on shared knowledge and creating positive social impact.
source_quote: push AI forward by building on each other’s knowledge and creating positive social impact
source_text:
At KickstartAI, we bring together people who want to push AI forward by building on each other’s knowledge and creating positive social impact.
As we move into 2026, we’re excited to continue our mission to accelerate AI adoption in the Netherlands and build on what we achieved in 2025:

🤝 Growing our community of AI professionals and strengthening knowledge sharing through hands-on sessions, workshops, expert blogs, and our podcast.

🚀 Opening up TechDays – our flagship AI event – to the wider Dutch ecosystem for the first time, creating space for organisations to exchange experiences and learn from one another.

⚙️ Staying focused on applying AI in real-world settings rather than in labs, we completed three projects with Ampère, Erasmus MC and Stichting Voedselbanken Nederland, and continued working with partners such as ING, KLM Royal Dutch Airlines, Nederlandse Spoorwegen, and Ahold Delhaize
on use cases ranging from detecting unattended objects at train stations to building a financial health chatbot and optimising fuel planning.

🏆 Co-organizing the National AI Challenge for the second year in a row with AI Coalitie 4 Nederland | AIC4NL, and working with the two winners – Leiden University Medical Center and Politie Nederland – on developing solutions for automated wound reporting and supporting the fight against environmental crime.

🏛️ Taking part in working visits with Dutch ministries to share insights on AI adoption.

Below is a glimpse of what we stand for 👇 

#AI #AINetherlands #AIAdoption #AICommunity

source_text:

📚 In what ways can AI support education?

In our latest Community Spotlight, Dr Pauldy Otermans and Dev Aditya, founders of Otermans Institute, describe how their work evolved from direct teaching in underserved communities to building conversational AI teachers deployed across multiple education levels.

They reflect on launching their first AI teacher in 2021, early deployments in refugee camps, expansion through partnerships with governments and institutions, and recent engineering work to enable on-device, offline operation in low-connectivity environments.
